q: A learner wants the highest-return daily habit from the 30-day brain-upgrade loop. Which block of the daily loop is described as the single biggest lever?
options: The RECALL block — daily spaced-repetition retrieval practice, run first thing
The PROMPT block — freewriting five lines on a single mind-expanding question
The ENCODE block — turning each day's key insight into brand-new flashcards
The DEEP WORK block — reading one advanced source in a focused twenty-minute sitting
explanation: The loop opens with RECALL precisely because active recall plus spacing is the most evidence-backed lever (Roediger & Karpicke; Ebbinghaus). The other blocks matter, but the chapter names daily retrieval as the #1 habit.

q: Why does the program treat sleep as a training input rather than as time taken away from studying?
options: A fixed sleep schedule mainly improves next-day mood, willpower, and motivation
Memory consolidation happens largely during sleep, so cutting it deletes the day's learning
Sleep restores eye strain and posture fatigue but plays no real role in memory
Extra sleep simply leaves a person with fewer hours available to review flashcards
explanation: Slow-wave and REM sleep drive hippocampal replay and consolidation (Walker, 2017; Rasch & Born, 2013). A sleep-deprived brain encodes far less and consolidates poorly, so protecting sleep protects gains already earned.

q: Which statement reflects the program's honest expectation-setting about results?
options: A validated IQ score can reliably be raised about fifteen points within the thirty days
Puzzle and brain-training apps dependably produce broad far transfer to general intelligence
Real gains are durable near-transfer skills plus repaired sleep and focus leaks that compound
Whatever improvement appears will vanish completely the very day after the program ends
explanation: The chapter rejects a fifteen-point IQ jump as snake oil and cites Owen et al. (2010) and Simons et al. (2016) on the absence of far transfer. The realistic payoff is near-transfer skill gains plus repaired sleep and focus habits.

q: What is the recommended way to use an AI assistant for the daily mind-expanding prompt?
options: Read the assistant's full answer first and then commit its wording to memory
Have the assistant summarize the whole chapter so the dense reading can be skipped
Use the assistant only to bulk-generate several hundred flashcards in one batch
Write your own answer first, then have the assistant critique it and find the weakest assumption
explanation: Passively reading an AI answer is re-reading in disguise. The high-leverage pattern is to write first, then let the model act as a Socratic critic that pushes back, and to respond to the gaps it surfaces.

q: According to the chapter and Dunlosky et al. (2013), which study tactic is among the least effective?
options: Highlighting passages and then re-reading the same source over and over again
Closed-book retrieval of the key ideas immediately after finishing the reading
Turning the main ideas into your own short question-and-answer style flashcards
Explaining the concept simply in your own words, Feynman-style, to expose gaps
explanation: Highlighting and re-reading feel productive but build little durable memory. The active-reading protocol replaces them with retrieval: close the source and write the key ideas from memory.

q: In the program's memory toolkit, which technique is matched to remembering ordered lists, sequences, or the steps of a process?
options: Spaced repetition driven by the FSRS scheduler built into modern Anki
The memory palace, also known as the method of loci, walked along a route
The major system, which maps each digit to a specific consonant sound
Elaboration combined with dual coding to enrich conceptual understanding
explanation: The chapter maps each tool to a job: memory palace for ordered lists and processes, major system for numbers, spaced repetition for long-term facts, and elaboration plus dual coding for conceptual understanding.

q: The thinking routine called Inversion is best summarized as which prompt?
options: Break an unknown quantity into factors, estimate each one, and multiply them
State the strongest possible version of an idea before you decide to reject it
Ask what would guarantee failure, then deliberately design those failure modes out
Attach an explicit probability to each prediction and later score your calibration
explanation: Inversion flips 'how do I succeed?' into 'what would guarantee failure?' The chapter suggests listing the top three ways a plan could fail before committing, so most flaws are caught in design rather than in production.

q: Using Keshav's three-pass method for a research paper, what is the goal of Pass 1?
options: Re-implement the paper, mentally or in code, and challenge every single assumption
Read the full body of the paper carefully, including all proofs and derivations
Read only the reference list to map out the related prior work and its lineage
Spend five to ten minutes skimming to grasp the paper's category, contribution, and claims
explanation: Pass 1 is a short five-to-ten-minute skim of title, abstract, intro, headings, and conclusion. Pass 2 reads the body while ignoring proofs, and the optional Pass 3 virtually re-implements and critiques the work.

q: Which flashcard-writing rule does the chapter give to keep a spaced-repetition deck effective?
options: Keep one fact per card and phrase the front as a question requiring active recall
Bulk-import large premade decks early so the collection grows as fast as possible
Add several multiple-choice options to every card to make daily review feel easier
Combine several closely related facts onto one card to cut down total review time
explanation: The minimum-information principle says one fact per card, phrased as a question that forces retrieval rather than recognition. Oversized or multiple-choice cards undermine the testing effect, and dreaded bulk imports get abandoned.

q: What is the intended role of the weekly full rest day and the scheduled walks and naps in the program?
options: They are optional rewards a learner may freely skip whenever motivation runs high
They are deliberate recovery that enables consolidation and prevents burnout erasing gains
They exist mainly to make the month's calendar look a little less demanding overall
They are meant to replace nightly sleep, so total sleep duration stops mattering much
explanation: The chapter frames recovery as a first-class training input: brief naps and wakeful rest aid consolidation, and a weekly rest day prevents burnout. Compounding requires recovery, and these do not substitute for nightly sleep.
